concerns.text: FR-004 requires selecting objects in the top '[deferred]' of saliency. The term '[deferred]' is a placeholder for a numeric value (e.g., 10%), not a valid selection criterion. A system cannot 'select objects whose average saliency falls in the top [deferred]' without a defined threshold. This contradicts the requirement for a deterministic system behavior.

FR-009 mandates a sensitivity analysis over thresholds '{low percentile..., [deferred], [deferred]}'. The set contains undefined placeholders. A sensitivity analysis requires specific, distinct values to compare. The requirement is internally contradictory as it demands a comparison of undefined values.

FR-002 mandates using the 'Deep Visual Attention Prediction model'. However, US-2 (Independent Test) and US-3 (Acceptance Scenario 2) reference a 'Vision-Transformer CAM saliency map' and 'ViT-B/16 CAM'. The spec fails to define the relationship between the primary model in FR-002 and the alternative model in US-3, creating ambiguity on whether FR-002's model is the only allowed one or if the ViT model is a valid alternative for the primary pipeline.

The Assumptions section contains two paragraphs that are actually detailed implementation logic (e.g., 'The system MUST derive these...', 'The power analysis... targets...'). These are not assumptions but functional requirements or constraints. This mixes terminology and scope, potentially obscuring the actual requirements in the FR section.

US-3 Acceptance Scenario 1 states: 'when the threshold is changed to 5 % or [deferred]'. The use of '[deferred]' as a valid input state in an acceptance scenario is inconsistent with the definition of a testable scenario. A test scenario must have a concrete state.
